metadata.title: Why Code That Looks Correct Can Still Be Wrong

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The line I can't shake is that correctness is an outcome property, not a surface property — the AI produces the surface, only the oracle produces the verdict, which is why HumanEval had to exist. Help me build the oracle habit: (1) name the external verifiers — tests, builds, type-checkers, linters — I should route AI-generated code through before trusting it; (2) explain why the symptom-fix-three-files-away failure slips past a plausible-looking read; (3) show me how to tell whether a task even has a clear oracle, since the productivity gains shrink without one.

props.command: The line I can't shake is that correctness is an outcome property, not a surface property — the AI produces the surface, only the oracle produces the verdict, which is why HumanEval had to exist. Help me build the oracle habit: (1) name the external verifiers — tests, builds, type-checkers, linters — I should route AI-generated code through before trusting it; (2) explain why the symptom-fix-three-files-away failure slips past a plausible-looking read; (3) show me how to tell whether a task even has a clear oracle, since the productivity gains shrink without one.
props.segment: Why Code That Looks Correct Can

beats.narration_text: A developer copied AI-fixed code into the codebase. Thirty seconds later the tests failed in a new way. The code looked correct. The system still broke.

production_viz.label: code looks right, tests still fail
production_viz.mechanic: a code chip (teal surface) on left; an arrow pointing to a test-fail chip (crimson) on right; below: 'looks correct' label under code chip, 'still fails' label under fail chip

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] code passes the eye test, fails the oracle

beats.narration_text: The developer had no way to tell from reading it. The fix addressed the symptom. The real bug lived three files away.
beats.scene_description: developer at terminal looking at a test failure output, concerned expression, code editor visible on screen; editorial newsprint desaturated treatment; slow push-in
beats.image_prompt: B02, developer at a terminal with a code editor open on screen, looking at test failure output with a concerned expression, editorial desaturated newsprint treatment, flat print lighting, no text legible, collage aesthetic
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] developer facing a test failure after AI-generated fix

beats.narration_text: The AI produced code that looks correct and runs without error. The system still breaks. Why can't the appearance of code tell you if it's right?

card.copy: Why can't the appearance of code tell you if it's right?

beats.narration_text: You might expect that code which reads correctly is correct code. Clean syntax, proper indentation, readable logic — these are signals that should predict correctness.

production_viz.label: naive model: appearance predicts correctness
production_viz.mechanic: a chip labeled 'LOOKS CORRECT' (teal) -> arrow -> chip labeled 'IS CORRECT' (teal); labeled 'the naive expectation'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the naive model — appearance implies correctness

beats.narration_text: Code correctness is not a surface property. It is an outcome property — established by running the code against external verification: tests, builds, type-checkers, linters. Mark Chen and colleagues introduced HumanEval precisely because code cannot be judged by appearance.

production_viz.label: the oracle chain: code -> run -> tests pass/fail
production_viz.mechanic: three chips in sequence: 'CODE' (slate) -> 'RUN ORACLE' (teal) -> 'VERDICT' (teal for pass, crimson for fail); the verdict chip is what matters, not the code surface

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the oracle chain — verdict, not surface

beats.narration_text: Fluency is not verification. An AI generates code by predicting plausible next tokens — it optimizes for text that looks like correct code. There is no internal mechanism that tests the code against your system before it outputs it.

production_viz.label: fluency vs verification: two different properties
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: left 'FLUENCY' column showing AI generating code chip that looks teal; right 'VERIFICATION' column showing oracle running and returning teal or crimson verdict; the columns are separate — fluency does not produce the right column

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] fluency and verification are separate properties

beats.narration_text: So code that looks correct and code that is correct are two different things — the first is a surface judgment, the second is an oracle judgment. The AI produces the surface. Only the oracle produces the verdict.

card.copy: Trust the oracle, not the output.

beats.narration_text: Productivity research on AI coding assistance found speedups on well-defined, self-contained tasks. The key variable is whether the task has a clear oracle. Without one, gains shrink and review burden grows — because you are judging code by whether it looks plausible.

production_viz.label: oracle present vs absent: different outcomes
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: left 'WITH ORACLE' showing code -> test run -> pass chip (teal); right 'NO ORACLE' showing code -> appearance judgment -> uncertain outcome (crimson question mark); speedup label on left, risk label on right

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] oracle present vs absent — different risk profiles

beats.narration_text: A data-cleaning function looks clean. AI resolves the KeyError — but introduces a silent off-by-one error on empty input. Three weeks later an empty dataset hits production. Zero exceptions, zero alerts, wrong output shipped to forty clients.

production_viz.label: off-by-one error invisible to appearance
production_viz.mechanic: two function boxes side by side labeled 'ORIGINAL' and 'AI FIX'; original has a red KeyError badge; AI fix has a teal surface — looks clean; but an orange EDGE CASE layer appears beneath the AI fix showing empty input path with wrong output; shipped to 40 clients label in crimson at bottom

beats.narration_text: Zero exceptions. Zero alerts. Wrong output shipped. The code that replaced it also looked correct. Neither the surface nor the AI's confidence is evidence of correctness.

card.copy: Appearance is not evidence. The oracle is the arbiter.

beats.narration_text: The practice is to run the oracle before accepting code. When you paste code into chat, you have no oracle. When you use an agentic coding tool that runs your tests, you do. The presence of an oracle changes the risk profile of the entire task.

production_viz.label: chat vs agent: oracle presence changes everything
production_viz.mechanic: two rows: top 'CHAT' row showing paste -> AI output -> no oracle (crimson X); bottom 'AGENT' row showing repo context -> AI edit -> oracle runs -> verdict (teal check); oracle presence is the distinguishing factor

beats.narration_text: The practice move: before accepting any AI-generated code, name your oracle. What test, build, or check will verify this? If you cannot name one, you are making an appearance judgment — and appearance is not evidence.

production_viz.label: the oracle gate: name it before accepting
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal flow: AI CODE -> ORACLE GATE (teal) -> ACCEPT; below the gate: a label 'what test/build verifies this?'; YES path goes right (teal), NO path points back (crimson)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the oracle gate — name it before accepting

beats.narration_text: For the developer: when Claude Code proposes a fix, she scopes the task to one file, runs the failing test, and checks that the full module test suite still passes. The oracle runs. The verdict is teal or crimson — not plausible.
beats.scene_description: developer at terminal watching tests run in a CI panel, focused and attentive, test results visible as a column of green check marks; editorial newsprint desaturated treatment; slow push-in
beats.image_prompt: B13, developer at terminal watching a test suite run with a column of results visible on screen, focused attentive expression, editorial desaturated newsprint treatment, flat print lighting, no text legible, collage aesthetic

beats.narration_text: The check: before accepting AI-generated or AI-fixed code, ask — what runs to verify this? Tests, build, type-check, lint. If the answer is 'it looks right,' that is an appearance judgment, not a verification.

production_viz.label: the check: name the oracle
production_viz.mechanic: a decision diamond: 'Can you name the oracle?' -> YES (teal) -> 'RUN IT'; NO (crimson) -> 'APPEARANCE JUDGMENT — STOP'; the YES path is highlighted

beats.narration_text: Code correctness is not a surface property. It is an outcome established by running an oracle. AI optimizes for surface. Only the oracle verifies the outcome.

card.copy: Trust the oracle, not the surface.

beats.narration_text: Code that looks correct can still be wrong — because correctness is an oracle verdict, not a visual property. Name the oracle before you accept the fix.

card.copy: Appearance is not evidence. Run the oracle.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Code correctness is not a surface property. It is an outcome established by running an oracle. AI optimizes for surface. Only the oracle verifies the outcome. Code that looks correct can still be wrong — because correctness is an oracle verdict, not a visual property. Name the oracle before you accept the fix.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why Code That Looks Correct Can Still Be Wrong] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
